title: Read so it sticks
description: SQ3R: turn a textbook chapter into knowledge, not eye exercise.
summaryPoints: Interrogate the text instead of letting your eyes slide over it
Run SQ3R: Survey, Question, Read, Recite, Review
Survey a chapter first to build a map before you dive in
Recite from memory after each section — that's the part that sticks
Highlight sparingly and write margin notes in your own words

content.title: Eyes moving isn't learning
content.body:
You read the whole page. You get to the bottom. You remember... nothing.

That's **passive reading** — eyes sliding over words while your brain naps. It feels like studying, but nothing lands.

**Active reading** is the opposite: you interrogate the text. You hunt for answers, argue with the author, and pull ideas into your own head instead of waiting for them to soak in.
content.imageAlt: An open book with a highlighter and floating question marks

content.prompt: If you finish every page on time, you've learned the chapter.
content.explanation: Finishing the pages just means your eyes moved. Learning shows up when you can recall and explain what you read.

content.title: Meet SQ3R
content.body:
There's a classic five-step method for chewing through a chapter: **SQ3R**.

- **S**urvey — skim first to build a map
- **Q**uestion — turn headings into questions
- **R**ead — read to answer them
- **R**ecite — say it from memory
- **R**eview — fill the gaps

Three of the steps start with R, hence "3R." Next we'll walk each one.

content.title: Survey: get the map first
content.body:
Before reading word-for-word, **skim the whole chapter** — headings, the intro, the summary, bold terms, and any figures.

This takes a few minutes and gives you a map. Now you know where the chapter is going, so each section has a place to slot into instead of arriving as a surprise.

Don't read deeply yet. You're scouting the terrain, not hiking it.

content.prompt: What should you do FIRST with a brand-new chapter?
content.context: It's 30 pages on cell biology and you've never opened it.
options: Start reading line one and don't stop till the end
Skim the headings, intro, summary, and bold terms
Highlight the first paragraph in three colors
Copy every definition into your notes
content.explanation: Surveying first builds a mental map, so every section you read later has somewhere to land instead of floating loose.

content.title: Question: turn headings into questions
content.body:
See a heading like "Causes of the French Revolution"? Flip it into a question: **"What caused the French Revolution?"**

Now you're reading with a target. Your brain perks up because it's looking for an answer, not just absorbing gray text.

Do this for every heading before you read that section. A question is a tiny hook your memory can hang the answer on.

content.instructions: One word is wrong. Tap it.
content.sentence: In the Question step you turn each heading into a statement before you read.
content.explanation: Turning a heading into a question gives your reading a target — you read to answer it.

content.title: Read and Recite
content.body:
**Read** one section at a time, hunting for the answer to your question. Slow down on the hard parts — it's not a race.

Then the step that actually builds memory: **Recite**. Look away and say or write the answer in your own words.

That's retrieval — pulling the idea out of your head. Rereading feels productive but barely sticks. Reciting is where the learning happens.

content.prompt: Which move builds the most lasting memory?
options: Rereading the section a second time
Looking away and saying the answer in your own words
content.explanation: Reciting from memory is retrieval, which forces your brain to rebuild the idea. Rereading and copying just feel busy.

content.prompt: Match each SQ3R letter to what you actually do.

content.title: Review, then annotate well
content.body:
**Review** last: go back, fill any gaps, and summarize the whole chapter in a few lines.

While reading, annotate smart:
- Highlight **sparingly** — only key terms and claims
- Write margin notes in your own words
- Mark confusing bits with a "?"
- Connect ideas to things you already know

If you highlight everything, nothing stands out. The whole page glows yellow and your eye has no clue what mattered.

content.prompt: Highlighting almost every sentence helps you remember more.
content.explanation: When everything is highlighted, nothing stands out. Highlight sparingly so the few key terms and claims actually pop.

content.prompt: Active reading habit or passive one?

items.text: Turning a heading into a question
Letting your eyes slide to the page's end
Marking a confusing part with a '?'

content.prompt: The step where you look away and say the answer in your own words is called ___.
content.hint: It's one of the three R's, and it means retrieval.
content.explanation: Recite (or recall) is the retrieval step — saying it from memory is what turns reading into knowledge.

content.prompt: Put the SQ3R steps in order.
content.explanation: Map the chapter, set questions, read for answers, recite from memory, then review to fill gaps — that's SQ3R.
